whereToStay: On-site camping is the only real option — this is a camping festival in its bones. Orange, Purple and White campsites are the general options, flat and close to the arena. Blue Camping is dedicated family/quiet camping. Pre-pitched tents sell out months in advance. The site is intentionally rural so there are no practical hotel alternatives nearby; Northampton (15 miles) is your nearest town.
survivalIntel: Shambala searches thoroughly and takes its no-single-use-plastic policy seriously — bring a reusable cup and water bottle. The Saturday fancy dress parade is one of the great UK festival moments; put effort into your costume. August bank holiday weather in Northamptonshire ranges from scorching to genuinely cold — pack layers for late nights. The secret is that the site is beautifully flat, which veteran festival-goers genuinely appreciate after a four-day weekend.
firstTimerScoreNote: Smaller, flatter and friendlier than almost any UK festival of comparable quality, with a crowd that actively looks after each other — just embrace the costume culture and you'll fit in immediately.

vibe: Hungary's vast psytrance gathering — 60,000 people on a dusty plain at DÃ¡dpuszta for a week of 24-hour psychedelic music, fire art, and tribal community. The crowd pulls hardcore psyheads from Europe, Israel, Brazil and beyond, mixing with growing numbers of first-timers and families drawn by the festival's legendary atmosphere. The main stage gets genuinely frightening by 3am. The lake, the Chill Dome hammocks, and the Dragon's Nest live stage offer respite from the relentless BPMs.
gettingThere: Fly into Budapest Liszt Ferenc (BUD) — it's the only sensible choice. The OzoraShuttle service picks up directly from the airport car park and drives you to the festival gates with no additional connections needed, typically costing â‚¬30—â‚¬40 return. Alternatively, taxi or public bus to Budapest Keleti or KelenfÃ¶ld stations, then train toward Simontornya station, then a shuttle or donation-based bus to the gates — allow 2.5—3 hours total and carry both euros and Hungarian forints.
whereToStay: Camping on-site is the only genuine option for the full experience — week-long tickets assume camping. Avoid pitching at the bottom of hills (flash flooding risk) and get as far from the main stage as you need to actually sleep. The music plays 24/7 and carries further than you'd expect. Budapest has excellent hostels and hotels if you want a recovery night at the end; it's about 90 minutes from the site.
survivalIntel: Hungary in late July and early August is brutally hot during the day — 35Â°C-plus is common. Dust is relentless; bring a neck gaiter or bandana for the main stage. At night it can drop sharply, so a sleeping bag rated below 10Â°C isn't overdoing it. Most importantly: the festival is massive. Arrange a specific meeting point with your group on day one because phone signal is patchy and 60,000 people all look like your friends.
firstTimerScoreNote: The logistics of getting to rural Hungary, surviving a week of extreme heat and 24-hour music, and navigating a 60,000-person psytrance city reward those who've done a few festivals first — but the community is genuinely welcoming if you're willing to do the prep.
gettingThereByTrain: Fly into Budapest, then take the 100E airport bus to the city (€5.50). From Kelenföld station take a train to Simontornya (around €10, check mav-start.hu). A donation-based community shuttle bus runs from Simontornya station to the festival gates — bring €5 in cash as a donation. Carry both euros and Hungarian forints.
whereToStayTips: On-site camping at Ozora is the only real option — this is a remote psytrance gathering in rural Hungary and there are no hotels nearby. Bring everything you need for 5+ days. Stay in Budapest before and after the festival — the ruin bar district in District VII is perfect for pre and post festival nights. Book a hostel in Budapest's Pest side for the best nightlife access.
survivalIntelExtra: Ozora is a marathon not a sprint — pace yourself across 5+ days. The main stage plays 24/7 so there's no rush to stay up all night on day one. Invest in a storm-proof tent — rain is almost guaranteed. Bring a tarp for daytime shade as the camping area has little tree cover. Padlock your tent. Flip flops for the hot daytime ground, warm layers for cold nights. The tea shack uphill from the main stage is a beloved community space — visit it for a slower moment.

vibe: Europe's premier bass music festival, built inside an actual Roman fort on a cliff above the Adriatic. Dubstep, DnB, roots, reggae and jungle collide in Fort Punta Christo near Pula, Croatia. The crowd is international, bass-literate and there for the music — but the setting means everyone's also slightly drunk on the fact that they're dancing in a 19th-century fortress overlooking the sea. Beach parties by day, underground tunnels and courtyard stages by night.
gettingThere: Pula Airport (PUY) is 20 minutes from the festival site and has direct summer flights from London, Manchester and several European hubs — the most straightforward option. Venice Marco Polo (VCE) is a 3—4-hour drive but has more flight options and cheaper fares; car hire groups work well for this crossing. From Pula Airport, taxis run about â‚¬20 to the site. The festival runs official shuttle buses from Pula city centre to Fort Punta Christo throughout the event.
whereToStay: The on-site campsite at Puntizella beach is basic but the experience is unbeatable — you wake up metres from the Adriatic. Budget for a proper lilo or air mattress as the ground is pebble and rock. For something softer, apartments in the Stinjan/FaÅ¾ana area (10—15 minutes' drive) are rented by locals specifically for the festival crowd and can be brilliant value split between six people. Book months in advance.
survivalIntel: Croatian August means ferocious heat in your tent — a heat-reflecting foil sheet costs £3 and will save your mornings. The sea is right there, swim in it, it will revive you. Bring a lilo for the beach stage or you'll be dancing on pebbles for days. The opening amphitheatre concert in Pula is a separate ticket event in a genuine 2,000-year-old Roman arena — it's one of the great festival experiences in Europe and worth every effort to get a ticket.
firstTimerScoreNote: Getting to Croatia adds complexity and cost, but the venue, the music, and the beach-plus-fortress combination make it a genuinely transformative first proper bass festival — just don't show up without a lilo and sunscreen.
gettingThereByTrain: Fly into Zadar or Split — both airports have pre-bookable festival transfer shuttles running to The Garden Tisno. Regular buses connect Split and Zadar to Tisno during the festival period. Night buses operate until 3am between the venue and Tisno village.
whereToStayTips: On-site accommodation at The Garden resort in Tisno is the best option — book directly through the Outlook website as it sells out fast. The resort has accommodation tiers from camping to apartments with sea views. Tisno village itself has apartments and guesthouses for a more local feel. Zadar and Split make great pre/post festival bases — both cities are stunning for a few days of Croatian coast before heading home.
survivalIntelExtra: Outlook is intimate by festival standards — the Garden resort setting means you're always close to the action. Bring reef-safe sunscreen for the boat parties. The boat party tickets sell out before the festival — book them the moment they go on sale. Tisno town is walkable from the venue for a quieter meal or coffee during the day. Carry small amounts of local kuna for village shops and markets.

vibe: A family-run hidden valley in the Herefordshire countryside that quietly gets on with being one of Britain's most beloved small festivals. Around 5,000 people — a genuine mix of families, ravers and everyone in between — spread across 11 stages including a Cabinet of Lost Secrets you access by climbing through a tiny door. The ethos is DIY, the drinks are cheap, and the crowd is overwhelmingly there to have a good time rather than be seen having one.
gettingThere: The nearest train stations are Hereford and Worcester; from either, head to Bromyard town centre (by bus or taxi). From Bromyard Pump Street, a free continuous shuttle bus runs directly to the festival from 10am until 2am each day. Prefer a direct ride? Coach services run from London and Bristol for around £18 each way. Driving: take the M5 Junction 7 or M50 Junction 2, then follow festival signs — postcode HR7 4LS. Hereford's Blueline Taxis offers a 10% discount; quote 'Nozstock' when booking.
whereToStay: Camping is on-site, adjacent to the festival entrance, and you can bring your own booze to the campersite — a rare and appreciated freedom. There's also a Van Haven for live-in vehicles. No real hotel options nearby; the festival is literally a farm in a valley. Bromyard has a few B&Bs for the less committed. Insider tip: the bars inside the festival are cash-only, so hit the ATM before you arrive or you'll pay £5 for the on-site machine.
survivalIntel: Herefordshire in July can produce genuine heat — this valley traps it. Pack sun cream alongside your waterproof; the weather can swing violently in either direction. The Cabinet of Lost Secrets stage is real and genuinely difficult to find; prioritise locating it on day one. Bring cash — bars are cash-only. The festival is genuinely intimate, which means sound bleeds between stages less than you'd expect and sightlines are almost always good.
firstTimerScoreNote: Small, well-organised, affordable, family-friendly without being square, and staffed by people who actually care — Nozstock is arguably the best UK festival for a first-timer who wants the full multi-stage experience without the overwhelming scale of the world's largest events.

vibe: Held at Jodrell Bank Observatory with the Lovell Telescope — Britain's largest — looming over the main stage. The crowd is a rare combination: serious music fans who also find astrophysics genuinely exciting. Indie, electronic and leftfield acts share billing with science talks, space installations and real-time telescope observations. Families are everywhere and welcome. It's the only festival where you'll walk from a headline set into a live Q&A with an astronaut.
gettingThere: The closest major airport is Manchester (MAN), just 26 minutes by car. Best train approach: London Euston to Macclesfield (1h 41m direct), then taxi or pre-arranged transfer to the site — about 15 minutes, roughly £15—£20. The festival recommends Macclesfield over Goostrey (technically nearer) because the roads from Goostrey station are unlit, narrow country lanes with no pavement at night. Advanced car park tickets are £31 for the weekend; buy online. Postcode for the site: SK11 9DW — not the standard Google listing.
whereToStay: Camping is on-site and the preferred option — pitching within sight of the Lovell Telescope is genuinely one of the more memorable things you can do at a UK festival. Arrive Thursday or early Friday to get a pitch near the main arena; the walk from far camping to the stage can be 40 minutes return. De Vere Cranage Estate and other Cheshire hotels are the practical non-camping alternative, 15—20 minutes' drive away.
survivalIntel: Jodrell Bank is an exposed Cheshire field — wellies and a waterproof are not optional, even in late July. There are no festival showers on-site, which surprises first-timers; pack dry shampoo and baby wipes. The campsite car park is 0.4 miles from pitches, so bring a trolley for your gear. Book the science talks and Space Dome shows in advance — they fill up fast and the astrophysics content is genuinely not to be missed if you make the trip.
firstTimerScoreNote: Compact, accessible from Manchester, and uniquely good at combining music with science in a way that never feels forced — the only real catch is the lack of onsite showers and the exposed Cheshire weather.

vibe: Four days inside the actual grounds of Belvoir Castle in Leicestershire — yes, a real castle, real battlements, real moat. The crowd skews young and hedonistic (it's explicitly 18+ and enforces it), but the gothic woodland setting pulls it away from generic EDM festival territory into something with genuine atmosphere. Dance music, electronic acts and a loyal 'Forbidden Family' of repeat attendees who treat it as a homecoming. The castle at night, lit up behind the stages, is the visual centrepiece.
gettingThere: Closest train station is Grantham (East Midlands Railway from London King's Cross, about 65 minutes), with regular Big Green Coach shuttle buses running to and from the festival site throughout the event. Big Green Coach also runs weekend return coaches from Birmingham, London Victoria, Manchester, Leeds, Liverpool, Nottingham and several other cities — carbon-neutral, priced competitively. Day return coaches run Saturday and Sunday only. Parking passes from £25 day, £35 weekend — must be pre-purchased, not available on the door. Belvoir Castle postcode: NG32 1PE. Taxis from Grantham cost approximately £35.
whereToStay: On-site camping is the right choice — the setting inside castle grounds is genuinely unique, and the no-re-entry policy means leaving for a hotel is impractical anyway. Pre-pitched 'Forbidden Retreat' tents sell out fast. On-site lockers are available to pre-book. The nearest village (Bottesford, about £16 in a taxi) has almost nothing — the site's own Engine Yard farm shop opens from 8am and is your best off-site food option.
survivalIntel: Strictly no re-entry once you're inside — triple-check your bag before crossing the threshold. It's a real forest on real castle grounds, which means uneven terrain, exposed tree roots, and mud that appears from nowhere overnight. Footwear matters more here than at most festivals. The event is completely cashless and there are no cash machines on site, so load your card up before arriving. Charge your phone; this is too good-looking a venue not to photograph.
firstTimerScoreNote: The no-re-entry rule and cashless-only policy can trip up newcomers, but the castle setting is so extraordinary and the production quality so high that first-timers who do their prep will have a genuinely memorable experience.
gettingThereByTrain: Train to Loughborough or East Midlands Parkway — official Forbidden Forest shuttles run from both stations to the site. Check the festival website for the latest shuttle timetables. Driving is possible with pre-booked parking but leaving on Sunday evening sees significant delays.
whereToStayTips: On-site camping is available — the forest setting makes it an atmospheric option. Glamping upgrades are available through the official festival website. Loughborough has Premier Inn and ibis hotels for non-campers within easy shuttle reach of the site.
survivalIntelExtra: The woodland setting is genuinely atmospheric — explore the forest stages and art installations during daylight before the evening sessions start. Bring a good headtorch for navigating the woodland paths at night. The forest floor is uneven — wear proper footwear. The festival has a strong focus on alternative and underground music — go in open-minded and discover something new.

vibe: A single Sunday in south London's Brockwell Park dedicated to funk, soul, jazz and disco — simultaneously the most laid-back and the most musically committed day festival in London. The crowd is Brixton-adjacent regulars, jazz nerds, soul crate-diggers and anyone who came for the vibes and stayed for Ezra Collective. No camping, no overnight chaos — just excellent music on a grass hill with a Herne Hill lido as your neighbour. Overwhelmingly joyful.
gettingThere: No flights necessary — this is central London. Herne Hill National Rail station is a 5-minute walk from the park gates and connects from Blackfriars, London Bridge and St Pancras. Brixton Victoria line tube is a 10—15 minute walk. Buses 3, 37, 59, 196, 322 and more all serve the park. There is no parking and no reason to drive. From outside London, train to London Bridge or Blackfriars then direct to Herne Hill. Budget around £5—£15 return in zones depending on your origin.
whereToStay: Single-day event, no camping. Stay anywhere in south London — Brixton, Herne Hill, Clapham and Peckham are all walking or one-stop distance. Book early: the May bank holiday weekend means London accommodation prices spike hard. Airbnb in Herne Hill or Brixton will save money over central London hotels and gets you a five-minute walk home after the final act.
survivalIntel: Food queues can stretch to 45 minutes at peak times — eat before the headliner windows or go early. The park can get genuinely crowded with sound bleeding between stages near the cross-stage sections, so pick your spot strategically. May weather in London is famously unreliable — bring a light layer and a packable rain jacket. Arrive early enough to secure a patch of grass near the stage you actually care about; latecomers end up watching from a hill.
firstTimerScoreNote: A single day, public transport only, central London, genuinely world-class soul and jazz lineup — this is the easiest possible festival recommendation for anyone who's never been to an outdoor music event and wants to start with something that can't really go wrong.
